section.title: The Line of Shem
section.sectionRationale: Marked off by its own heading, the account of Shem, this genealogy is a deliberate bridge from the scattered nations of Babel to the calling of Abram. In ten measured generations it narrows the whole human story down to one family, tracing the covenant line from Shem to Terah, the father of Abram.
section.thesis: After Babel's scattering, God quietly threads the story down a single family line, Shem to Abram, showing that His plan to bless the nations will run through one chosen family.
section.frameNote: A ten-generation genealogy in a steady, repeating pattern, moving from Shem to Eber and on through Peleg to Terah and his sons, ending on the threshold of Abram's call.

verses.text: This is the {{toledot|account}} of Shem. Two years after the flood, when Shem was 100 years old, he became the father of Arphaxad.
And after he had become the father of Arphaxad, Shem lived 500 years and had other sons and daughters.
When Arphaxad was 35 years old, he became the father of Shelah.
And after he had become the father of Shelah, Arphaxad lived 403 years and had other sons and daughters.
When Shelah was 30 years old, he became the father of {{eber|Eber}}.
And after he had become the father of Eber, Shelah lived 403 years and had other sons and daughters.
When Eber was 34 years old, he became the father of Peleg.
And after he had become the father of Peleg, Eber lived 430 years and had other sons and daughters.
When Peleg was 30 years old, he became the father of Reu.
And after he had become the father of Reu, Peleg lived 209 years and had other sons and daughters.
When Reu was 32 years old, he became the father of Serug.
And after he had become the father of Serug, Reu lived 207 years and had other sons and daughters.
When Serug was 30 years old, he became the father of Nahor.
And after he had become the father of Nahor, Serug lived 200 years and had other sons and daughters.
When Nahor was 29 years old, he became the father of Terah.
And after he had become the father of Terah, Nahor lived 119 years and had other sons and daughters.
When Terah was 70 years old, he became the father of {{abram|Abram}}, Nahor, and Haran.

toledot.gloss: Generations, account, or family record. This heading word structures the whole book of Genesis, dividing it into ten sections. Here the account of Shem signals a fresh, purposeful movement: after the sprawl of Babel, the camera narrows to one line that carries the promise forward.

eber.gloss: The ancestor from whom the term Hebrew is usually traced. His placement in the line marks the branch that leads to Abram the Hebrew and to Israel, quietly identifying which family God is preparing to call.

abram.gloss: The name Abram means exalted father, later changed by God to Abraham, father of a multitude. The genealogy ends on his name like a held breath, for with him the story turns from the scattered nations to the one man through whom all nations will be blessed.

basic.context: Right after Babel, where people were scattered into many nations, the Bible slows down and follows just one family line. It lists ten generations from Shem, Noah's son, down to Terah, the father of Abram. It is like a spotlight narrowing from the whole world onto one household.
basic.hermeneutics: The key question is why the Bible bothers with a list of names and ages. The answer is that God is keeping a promise: He is tracing the family through whom He will bless the world. The list ends on Abram, setting up the great turning point of the next chapter.
basic.originalLanguages: The word account, toledot, is a heading that shapes all of Genesis and signals a purposeful new chapter in the story. Eber is the name behind the word Hebrew, marking the chosen branch. And the list lands on Abram, whose name means exalted father, the man God is about to call.
basic.typology: This quiet genealogy is a link in a much longer chain that runs all the way to Jesus. Matthew and Luke pick up these very names in their family trees of Christ. God preserves a faithful line through ordinary births and deaths until the promised Saviour is born.
basic.christ: Every name here is a step toward Jesus. The line from Shem to Abram continues to David and finally to Christ, so this list is part of the family tree of the Saviour. God is faithfully carrying His promise across the generations toward the one who fulfils it.
basic.god: God is patient and faithful, working across long stretches of time through unremarkable people. He does not abandon the world after Babel; He quietly keeps a line alive to bless it. He rules over births, lifespans, and generations to accomplish His plan.
basic.crossReferences: Luke's genealogy of Jesus runs back through these very names to Shem and beyond. First Chronicles repeats the line, and Joshua remembers that Terah's family once served other gods before God called Abram out.
basic.oneStory: After the world scatters at Babel, God does not start over with the whole earth; He starts with one family. This list is the thread of grace running from Shem to Abram, and on to Jesus, showing that God's rescue of the nations comes through one chosen line He faithfully preserves.

context.literary: This is one of the ten toledot sections that structure Genesis, the account of Shem. It mirrors the genealogy of Genesis 5, moving in a steady formula of birth, years, and further children, and it deliberately bridges the scattering of Babel and the call of Abram, narrowing from all nations to one line.
context.historical: The names locate the ancestral family in the world of Mesopotamia, ending with Terah in the region of Ur and Haran. The recorded lifespans are notably shorter than those before the flood, showing a gradual decline as the narrative moves from the primeval age toward the patriarchs.
context.canonical: The line is picked up in 1 Chronicles 1 and in Luke's genealogy of Jesus, which traces the Saviour back through Terah, Shem, and Noah to Adam. This makes the passage a load-bearing link in the single genealogy of redemption that spans the whole Bible.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bridge Babel and the call of Abram by tracing the chosen line from Shem to Terah, showing that God is faithfully preserving one family through whom His promise of blessing will come.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is descriptive record, not command. Its abiding truth is God's patient faithfulness across generations and His sovereign steering of history toward the promised seed.

debates.question: Do these genealogies give a complete, gap-free chronology of the years since the flood?

views.label: A precise chronology
views.case: Some read the ages strictly and add them to date the flood and creation, taking the list as a continuous, unbroken timeline.

views.label: A selective, telescoped line
views.case: Others note that biblical genealogies can skip generations and are shaped for theological ends, so the list traces a true line without necessarily giving every link or an exact total of years.

debates.bearing: Either way the theological point is secure: God preserves a real, traceable line from Shem to Abram, and the passage's purpose is the promise, not primarily a calendar.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the weight. The heading toledot, account, marks this as a fresh, deliberate movement in Genesis, not a random list. Eber sits in the line as the name behind Hebrew, quietly flagging the chosen branch. And the whole genealogy is drawn like an arrow toward Abram, exalted father, whose name God will soon enlarge to Abraham, father of a multitude, so that the list ends poised on the very edge of the promise.

typology.type: The preserved covenant line from Shem to Abram
typology.fulfillment: One strand of the single genealogy of redemption that runs to Christ, taken up by name in Luke 3.

typology.type: Abram, the exalted father the list awaits
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled as father of many nations and, through his seed, the source of blessing in Jesus, Galatians 3.

typology.type: God's faithfulness across ten silent generations
typology.fulfillment: Points to the God who keeps covenant to a thousand generations and sends His Son in the fullness of time, Galatians 4:4.

study.christ: This list is a section of Jesus' own family tree. Luke traces the Saviour back through Terah, Serug, Eber, Shelah, Arphaxad, and Shem to Noah and Adam, so every name here is a stepping stone toward Bethlehem. The line narrows from all the scattered nations to one family precisely so that from that family the Christ can come who will bless every nation again. God's patient bookkeeping of births and years is the slow, sure approach of the promised seed.
study.god: God shows Himself faithful over long, quiet years. After the drama of Babel He works not with spectacle but with ordinary families, sons and daughters, lifespans and deaths, steadily keeping His purpose alive. He is sovereign over generations, unhurried yet certain, and He does not forget His promise to bless the nations even as the human story shrinks to a single household waiting in Mesopotamia.

crossReferences.note: The genealogy ends on Abram; the next verses record God's call and promise to bless all nations through him.
Jesus' genealogy runs back through Terah, Nahor, Serug, Reu, Peleg, Eber, Shelah, Arphaxad, and Shem.
Terah's family served other gods beyond the River before God graciously called Abram out.
The same line from Shem to Abram is preserved as part of Israel's foundational record.

study.oneStory: When the nations scatter at Babel, the story could have ended in confusion. Instead God narrows the focus to one family and follows it, name by name, from Shem to Abram. This genealogy is the hidden hinge between the judgment of Babel and the promise to Abram, the thread of grace that God will draw all the way to Jesus, through whom the scattered nations are finally blessed and gathered.

quiz.q: Why does the Bible include this list of names right after Babel?

options.text: To narrow the story to the one family through whom God will bless the nations.
options.note: The genealogy bridges scattered Babel and the call of Abram.

options.text: To prove exactly how many years have passed since creation.
options.note: Some read it for chronology, but its main purpose is the promise, not a calendar.

options.text: To fill space with names before the next story.
options.note: The list is purposeful, tracing the covenant line to Abram.

options.text: To record Israel's family history for later generations.
options.note: True in part, but the deeper aim is to point toward the promised line.

quiz.q: On whose name does this genealogy end?

options.text: Abram, whom God is about to call.
options.note: The list lands on Abram, setting up the turning point of chapter twelve.

options.text: Noah, the father of the line.
options.note: The line begins from Shem, Noah's son, but it ends on Abram.

options.text: Terah alone, with no sons named.
options.note: Terah's sons are named, and the focus falls on Abram.
Nahor is named, but the spotlight rests on Abram.

quiz.q: How does this genealogy connect to Jesus?

options.text: It is part of Jesus' family tree, traced by name in Luke 3.
options.note: Luke runs Jesus' line back through these very names.

options.text: It has no link to Jesus at all.
options.note: These names appear directly in the genealogy of Christ.

options.text: It lists people who all personally knew Jesus.
options.note: They lived long before Jesus; the link is ancestry, not acquaintance.

options.text: It shows the line God preserved on the way to Christ.
options.note: True and central, though the sharpest point is that Luke names these very people.

quiz.q: What does the passage reveal about how God works?

options.text: He works patiently across generations through ordinary families.
options.note: God keeps His promise over long, quiet stretches of time.

options.text: He only acts through dramatic, public miracles.
options.note: Here He works quietly through births and deaths, not spectacle.

options.text: He abandoned the world after Babel.
options.note: The opposite; He preserves a line to bless the world.

options.text: He carries His purpose steadily toward the promised seed.
options.note: True and close to the heart of it, alongside His patient use of ordinary people.

questions: This whole chapter of the story is carried by ordinary people having children and growing old. How does it encourage you that God works through unremarkable, faithful lives like yours?
God kept His promise across ten generations before Abram was even born. Where do you need patience to trust that God is still working, even when nothing seems to be happening?
The line ends poised on Abram, right before God calls him. Where might God be quietly preparing you for something you cannot yet see?
Every name here is a step toward Jesus. How does it move you to know that God was working through history for centuries to bring the Saviour to you?
Terah's family once served other gods before God called them out. What in your family story has God been graciously redirecting toward Himself?

translationNotes.note: The Greek Old Testament, followed by Luke 3:35-36, inserts an extra generation, Cainan, between Arphaxad and Shelah, and gives longer lifespans; the differences show that these genealogies were transmitted in more than one form.
The heading this is the account of Shem uses the toledot formula that structures the whole of Genesis, marking a new and deliberate section rather than a mere list.
